{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.4, "R1.2": 0.6, "R1.5": 1.0, "R1.6": 0.3}}

Reordered the section so it flows setup → prior work → contribution → results, and added two small connective edits so the new order reads cleanly.

The original paragraph order is setup, prior work, results, contribution. That puts the empirical numbers before the reader has been told what method produced them, and it pushes the contribution statement (which the results paragraph already refers back to) to the end. The natural IMRaD-style flow for a section like this is: (1) problem setup, (2) prior work and its limitations, (3) the proposed rule and its stability guarantee, (4) experimental validation. Below is the artifact with paragraphs reordered to that sequence, plus minor transition adjustments where the old wording assumed the previous ordering.

---

We consider stabilization of a partially observed linear time-invariant plant under sensor scheduling. The dynamics follow x_{k+1} = A x_k + B u_k + w_k, with w_k zero-mean Gaussian process noise of covariance Q. At each step k the controller activates exactly one of m candidate sensors, each yielding y_k^{(i)} = C_i x_k + v_k^{(i)} at communication cost c_i. We assume (A, B) is stabilizable and that each pair (A, C_i) is detectable, so that any single-sensor schedule admits a finite steady-state Kalman filter.

Prior work on sensor scheduling has either solved the open-loop selection problem via dynamic programming over the Riccati recursion (Vitus et al., 2012) or relaxed it to a convex program over time-averaged activation frequencies (Joshi and Boyd, 2009). Value-of-information criteria from estimation theory have also been ported to switched control settings (Krishnamurthy, 2016). These approaches typically compute the schedule offline and assume the cost structure is fixed a priori.

The principal contribution of this section is a closed-form one-step lookahead rule, which we call regret-weighted EVPI, that selects the sensor maximizing the expected reduction in LQG cost-to-go relative to a cheapest-sensor default. We further establish, in Proposition 4, that under bounded process noise the rule yields an exponentially stable closed-loop with explicit decay rate rho < 1 depending only on (A, B, Q) and the minimum sensor cost. The rule, defined in Eq. (14), admits an O(m n^2) per-step implementation and is fully state-feedback rather than open-loop. Together with the stability certificate of Proposition 4 it is what produces the quantitative gains reported below; without these components the method collapses to standard certainty-equivalence control.

We evaluate the rule on a four-tank benchmark (Johansson, 2000) and on a quadrotor altitude-hold task with three candidate altimeters. The method achieves a 23% reduction in mean tracking error against the round-robin baseline and a 17% reduction against the greedy variance-minimization rule of Mo et al. (2014), under matched communication budgets. Closed-loop trajectories remained bounded across all 200 randomly initialized trials, and the empirical cost-to-go matched the LQG lower bound to within 4% on the linearized four-tank model. A representative disturbance-rejection trajectory is shown in Figure 3, and Table 2 reports the per-budget breakdown.

---

Notes on the small wording changes I made to support the reordering:

- In the (now third) contribution paragraph I changed "tabulated in the preceding paragraph" to "reported below," since the results now follow rather than precede it. I also rewrote the closing sentence to drop the backward reference and instead point forward to the results.
- In the (now fourth) results paragraph I added a one-clause opener ("We evaluate the rule on…") so it no longer starts mid-claim with "On a four-tank benchmark…"; the previous version relied on the reader having just read about benchmarks, which is no longer the case in the new order.

One thing I did not change but flag for your attention: the contribution paragraph claims a stability decay rate that depends "only on (A, B, Q) and the minimum sensor cost," yet the rule selects among sensors with different (C_i, v_k^{(i)}). It is worth confirming that the C_i and measurement-noise covariances genuinely drop out of the rho bound in Proposition 4; if they do not, the wording should be tightened. This is outside the restructuring brief, so I have left the sentence as written.
